content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1 428 ,I Street• Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
{916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Gary W. Schons 
Best Best & Krieger LLP 
655 West Broadway, 15th Floor 
San Diego, CA 9210 I 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-129 
Dear Mr. Schons: 
June 15, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Palm Desert Ma(or Jan 
Hamik regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and 
Section 1090. Please note that we do not advise on any other area oflaw, including Public Contract 
Code or common law conflicts of interest. Please also note that we are not a finder of fact when 
rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your 
facts are complete and accurate. 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Riverside County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097. l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097. l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1 (c)(S).) 
QUESTION 
Does Section I 090 prohibit Mayor Harnik from making or participating in the making of, or 
the City from entering into, a development agreement between the City and the applicant developer 
if the developer hires an attorney who rents office space from the Mayor and her husband? 
CONCLUSION 
Because the remote interest set forth in Section 109l(b)(5) applies, the City may enter into 
the development agreement, but the Mayor must abstain from making or participating in the making 
of decisions relating to that agreement. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-17-129 
Page No. 2 
You are the Palm Desert City Attorney and the authorized representative of Mayor Jan 
Hamik. The Mayor's husband is an attorney and practices law through a law corporation, Brian S. 
Harnik, Inc., which is a member of the law firm of Roemer & Hamik LLP. Roemer & Hamick LLP 
is located in Indian Wells. 
The Mayor and her husband are the owners of a professional office building in Indian Wells, 
California. Robert A. Bemheimer, APLC, is an attorney practicing through a professional law 
corporation. Attorney Bernheimer is a tenant of the Mayor and her husband's office building, and 
pays them rent in excess of $25,000 per year. Attorney Bernheimer does not work for, nor has any 
involvement in, Mr. Hamik's law corporation or Roemer & Harnik LLP. And Mr. Hamik has no 
involvement in Robert A. Bemheimer, Inc. 
A developer has applied to the City for approval of a multi-million-dollar, multiphase real 
estate development project within the City. If the City approves the project, the City and the 
developer will enter into a development agreement. The project-review process will require 
multiple, serial decisions by the City Council over time as the project moves through the process. 
Some of those decisions could significantly impact the viability of the project and the applicant 
developer's need for legal representation. Attorney Bernheimer is in discussions with the developer 
to represent the developer before the City in this process. 
You have asked whether Section 1090 would prohibit the Mayor from making or 
participating in the making of, or the City from entering into, the development agreement if the 
developer hires Attorney Bemheimer to represent the developer before the City in the project­
review process. 
ANALYSIS 
Section I 090 prohibits a public official from being financially interested in a contract in both the 
official's public and private capacities. (Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47Cal.4th 1050, 1073.) A 
contract that violates Section I 090 is void, regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair 
and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646-649.) When Section 1090 
is applicable to one member of a governing body of a public entity, the prohibition cannot be 
avoided by having the interested board member abstain; the entire governing body is precluded 
from entering into the contract. (Id. at pp. 647-649.) 
We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether a public officer or employee has a 
disqualifying conflict of interest under Section l 090. These steps are: (1) does the matter involve a 
public officer or employee; (2) does the matter involve a contract; (3) is the official making or 
participating in making a contract; (4) does the official have a financial interest in the contract; (5) 
does a remote or noninterest exception apply; and, under appropriate facts, (6) does the rule of 
necessity apply? 


File No. A-17-129 
Page No. 3 
Steps one through three are not at issue here. A public officer is involved because the Mayor 
is a public officer. A contract is involved because a development agreement between a city and an 
applicant is a contract for purposes of Section 1090.2 (McCann Advice Letter, No. A-16-184; 
Morrison Advice Letter, No. 14-132; 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230 (1995).) Because an officer is 
presumed to participate in the making of his or her agency's contracts when the officer is a member 
of a board or commission that has the authority to execute the contract at issue (Thomson, supra, p. 
649), the Mayor will be presumed to have participated in the making of the development agreement. 
Steps Four and Five: Does the Mayor have an interest in the potential development agreement 
for purposes of Section 1090? 
Section 1090 attempts to prevent honest government agents from succumbing to temptation 
by making it illegal for them to enter into relationships which are fraught with temptation. 
(Thomson, supra, at p. 648, citing Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 570.) For purposes of 
Section l 090, the term "financial interest" includes both direct and indirect interests in a contract. 
(See Id. at p. 645, citing Moody v. ShufJleton (1928) 203 Cal. 100.) 
Although the statute does not specifically define "financial interest," an examination of case 
law and the statutory exceptions to the basic prohibition indicate that the term is to be liberally 
interpreted. (See People v. Dasher (1934) 2 Cal.2d 141, 146.) "The defining characteristic of a 
prohibited financial interest is whether it has the potential to divide an official's loyalties and 
compromise the undivided representation of the public interests the official is charged with 
protecting." (Lexan, supra, at p. 1075.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at actual 
impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." ( City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey 
(1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
The Legislature, however, has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's 
prohibition where the interest involved is deemed a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091, or 
a "noninterest," as defined in Section 1091.5. The definitions of the remote and noninterest 
exceptions should be consulted for guidance to determine what falls within the scope of the term 
"financial interest" as used in Section 1090. (See 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34 (2002).) 
Section 1091 (b )( 5) provides that a public officer who is "a landlord or tenant of the 
contracting party" has a remote interest in that contract. The potential development agreement 
between the City and the applicant developer is the public contract at issue, and the developer is the 
contracting party for purposes of this exception. Attorney Bemheimer is a tenant of the Mayor and 
her husband's office building, and pays them rent in excess of $25,000 per year. Based on the facts 
2 Sections 65864 to 65869 .5 of the Planning and Zoning Law authorize a city or county to enter into a 
development agreement with an applicant for a development project. The Legislature authorized cities and counties to 
use development agreements to foster greater certainty, efficiency, and private participation in comprehensive planning, 
and to enable cities and counties to incentivize applicants to finance public facilities. (Section 65864.) Among other 
things, a development agreement must specify its duration, the pennitted uses of the property, the density or intensity of 
use, and the maximum height and size of proposed buildings; the development agreement may include conditions, 
tenns, restrictions, and requirements, as specified, including tenns and conditions relating to the applicant financing 
necessary public facilities over time. (Section 65865.2.) A development agreement is enforceable by any party to the 
agreement. (Ibid.) 


File No. A-17-129 
Page No. 4 
presented, this office-space lease is the only financial connection between Attorney Bemheimer or 
his professional corporation and the Mayor, her husband, or their various businesses. 
Because Attorney Bernheimer would be representing the applicant developer directly before 
the City, Attorney Bernheimer would be an agent of the developer, and therefore would be able to 
stand in the developer's shoes for purposes of Section 109l(b)(5)'s remote interest here. If the 
developer was the Mayor's tenant, this remote interest would clearly apply; we find it logical to 
extend application of this remote interest where the Mayor's interest in Attorney Bemheimer is 
even more remote. 3 Your facts provide no indication of financial interaction between Attorney 
Bernheimer and the Mayor or her husband beyond the office-space lease, and the Mayor's sole 
interest in Attorney Bernheimer as a tenant does not warrant prohibiting the City from consideration 
of the project. Therefore, we find that the remote interest set forth in Section 1091(b){5) applies. 
Thus, the City may enter into the development agreement, but the Mayor must abstain from 
making or participating in the making of decisions relating to that agreement. Accordingly, we do 
not analyze the applicability of the rule of necessity, and this Section 1090 analysis ends here. We 
also do not analyze the applicability of the Act's conflict of interest provisions to these decisions 
because the Mayor's abstention from them pursuant to Section 1091(b)(5) would also satisfy the 
Act's disqualification requirements set forth in Section 87105. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Jack Woodside 
Acting General Counsel 
- 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division 
3 In the Craft Advice Letter, No. A-14-168, we found that Section I 090 would not prohibit a candidate for city 
council from taking a seat on the city council if elected because the remote interest set forth in Section I 091 (b )( 1) for an 
officer or employee of a nonprofit entity would also apply to the candidate's spouse who was the executive director of a 
501(c)(3) nonprofit organization that had an existing contract with the city. If the city council candidate was elected to a 
seat on the city council, the city council could enter into a new contract with the 501(c)(3) organization so long as the 
councilmember abstained from making or participating in the making of that contract in accordance with the 
requirements of Section I 091 (a). As was the case in the Craft Letter, the extension of a remote interest is appropriate 
where the interest at issue is no less remote.

the City from entering into, a development agreement between the City and the applicant developer 
if the developer hires an attorney who rents office space from the Mayor and her husband?

Because the remote interest set forth in Section 109l(b)(5) applies, the City may enter into 
the development agreement, but the Mayor must abstain from making or participating in the making 
of decisions relating to that agreement.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does Section I 090 prohibit Mayor Harnik from making or participating in the making of, or 
the City from entering into, a development agreement between the City and the applicant developer 
if the developer hires an attorney who rents office space from the Mayor and her husband?

CONCLUSION: Because the remote interest set forth in Section 109l(b)(5) applies, the City may enter into 
the development agreement, but the Mayor must abstain from making or participating in the making 
of decisions relating to that agreement.
